title: Tapping for What Do I Believe
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the discomfort of not knowing what you believe any more
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the feeling of being spiritually unmoored: the loss of certainty, the tradition that no longer fits, the question without an answer. Taps through the discomfort of not knowing and toward accepting that the question itself is a valid place to be.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the question of what you believe is creating anxiety, grief, or a sense of being unmoored — particularly when childhood faith no longer fits.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the discomfort of spiritual uncertainty: not knowing what you believe, and the feeling that everyone else has an answer you don't. The sequence works on the discomfort of the gap, not the question itself. Not knowing is a real spiritual position. This sequence helps you stay in it without the anxiety running the show.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I don't know what I believe and it makes me feel unmoored, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though the tradition I inherited doesn't fit anymore and I don't know what does, I accept that this is where I am right now.
Even though the uncertainty is uncomfortable and I can't resolve it, I am willing to stay in the question.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: I don't know what I believe.
Side of Eye: The certainty other people seem to have that I don't.
Under Eye: What I was taught as a child doesn't fit anymore.
Under Nose: The gap where something used to be.
Chin: The discomfort of not having an answer.
Collarbone: The question I can't resolve.
Under Arm: The feeling that I should know by now.
Top of Head: Being between beliefs and not knowing how to be here.
Reframe
Eyebrow: Not knowing is a real and valid spiritual position.
Side of Eye: Many people live in the question and do so with integrity.
Under Eye: It is possible that the old form doesn't fit because I've grown past it.
Under Nose: The gap is not emptiness. It is space.
Chin: I can practice without certainty. The practice itself is a form of looking.
Collarbone: The question I carry is part of a spiritual life, not the absence of one.
Under Arm: I don't need to resolve this to live with integrity.
Top of Head: The search itself is the spiritual life.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
